title: VeriSign Inc. (VRSN) Economic Prospect Score — Consensus Analysis
description: Consensus economic prospect analysis of VeriSign Inc. (VRSN), averaging independent scores from Opus 4.6 and Gemini 3.1 across competitive momentum, moat durability, and sentiment catalysts.
verdictDetail: VeriSign operates a virtually unmatched regulatory monopoly as the sole registry for .com and .net top-level domains. This unique position grants it an incredibly capital-light business model with astonishing profit margins near 50%. While volume growth is maturing (7.6% revenue growth), consistent contractually allowed price increases ensure steady cash flow generation.

competitiveMomentum.title: Competitive Momentum
competitiveMomentum.summary: Dependable, contracted pricing power but sluggish organic volume growth.

factors.name: Revenue Growth vs. Peers
factors.rationale: VeriSign's revenue growth is solid (7.6%), but it largely stems from systematic price increases rather than a rapidly expanding customer base. The market for new .com domains is maturing.

factors.name: Market Share Trajectory
factors.rationale: As the exclusive registry for .com and .net domains, its market share for these critical web real estate assets is 100%. While alternative TLDs exist, .com remains the undisputed global standard.

factors.name: Pricing Power
factors.rationale: The ICANN contract explicitly allows VeriSign to raise prices on .com domains by up to 7% in four out of every six years. Because domain registration fees are tiny compared to the value of a business's online presence, these increases are absorbed without friction.

factors.name: Product Velocity
factors.rationale: Product innovation is virtually non-existent. VeriSign's core function is operating the DNS infrastructure securely; there is little need or opportunity for new product velocity.

moatDurability.title: Moat Durability
moatDurability.summary: An untouchable regulatory monopoly supported by critical global internet infrastructure.

factors.name: Switching Costs
factors.rationale: Switching away from a .com domain is unthinkable for an established business, as it would destroy brand equity, SEO rankings, and customer trust. The cost to retain the domain is negligible in comparison.

factors.name: Network Effects
factors.rationale: The .com domain benefits from an entrenched psychological network effect. Because it is the default top-level domain globally, new businesses overwhelmingly prefer it, reinforcing its dominance.

factors.name: Regulatory & IP Position
factors.rationale: The moat is entirely defined by regulatory fiat. Its exclusive cooperative agreement with the U.S. Department of Commerce and its contract with ICANN grant it an absolute legal monopoly.

factors.name: Capital Intensity Advantage
factors.rationale: VeriSign's infrastructure scales incredibly well. The cost of adding a new domain to the registry is near zero, resulting in a capital-light model that produces software-like profit margins approaching 50%.

sentimentCatalyst.title: Sentiment & Catalysts
sentimentCatalyst.summary: A cash flow machine, though highly dependent on future ICANN contract renewals.

factors.name: Earnings Estimate Revisions
factors.rationale: Earnings are highly predictable due to the recurring nature of domain renewals and contractual price hikes. Revisions are generally upward and very stable.

factors.name: News & Narrative Sentiment
factors.rationale: The narrative is polarized between investors who praise its unassailable monopoly and those who worry about slowing internet domain growth in China and the proliferation of alternative TLDs.

factors.name: Management & Capital Allocation
factors.rationale: Management executes a straightforward but effective strategy: operate the infrastructure flawlessly and return virtually all free cash flow to shareholders via consistent, aggressive share repurchases.

keyRisks: Regulatory risk is existential. If the U.S. government or ICANN alters the exclusive registry contract, the moat would evaporate.
A structural slowdown in net new domain registrations, particularly driven by macroeconomic weakness in key international markets like China.
The increasing viability of alternative top-level domains or walled gardens (e.g., social media pages) reducing the primacy of the .com brand.
keyCatalysts: Execution of the permitted 7% price increases under the current .com registry agreement, directly expanding operating margins.
Continued aggressive share repurchase programs supported by near-100% free cash flow conversion.
A rebound in international domain registration volume driven by expanding internet penetration in emerging markets.
methodology: Consensus Analysis — Economic Prospect Score averaging independent evaluations from Opus 4.6 and Gemini 3.1. Gemini scored VRSN at 83/100 and Opus at 76/100. Each factor score is the arithmetic mean of both models. Three pillars: Competitive Momentum (0-35), Moat Durability (0-35), and Sentiment & Catalysts (0-30).
